source.title: Play fighting and the development of the social brain: The rat's tale

confidence_rationale: Canonical, coherent rat model in a top review venue. Directly establishes the INV-075 antecedent for the candidate cluster: juvenile play is a developmentally self-extinguishing signal whose absence produces persistent adult socio-cognitive deficit with anatomical mPFC change -- i.e. it is load-bearing. Confidence capped below 0.8 because the leap from 'play tunes prefrontal social-cognitive competence' to 'play calibrates the three BG commitment loops' is a REE-side architectural extrapolation the paper does not itself make.
failure_signatures: Play does not train specific motor actions; benefit is a transferable skill set deployed across social and non-social contexts -- a substrate that hard-codes play-acquired motor policies rather than a domain-general commitment-calibration would mispredict this.

Benefit is tied to the juvenile period specifically; deprivation outside that window does not reproduce the deficit, so a model with no bounded developmental window for commitment calibration is unsupported.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Depriving rats of peer play during the juvenile period yields adults with socio-cognitive deficiencies correlated with physiological and anatomical changes in the medial prefrontal cortex; play improves a general, transferable skill set rather than specific motor actions.
mapping.ree_translation: Juvenile play is an INV-075-antecedent signal: self-extinguishing (play wanes with maturation) AND load-bearing (the socio-cognitive/commitment competence it establishes must persist into adulthood). The transferable-skill-set finding maps onto REE's claim that what play calibrates is not stored motor policies but the cross-context arbitration of the three commitment loops (ARC-021/MECH-069).
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper measures prefrontal social-cognitive competence, not basal-ganglia commitment-loop gains. 'Commitment-loop calibration' is the REE interpretation; the paper supports the antecedent (self-extinguishing + load-bearing developmental signal) but is silent on the specific loop-level mechanism and on whether the persistence is achieved by a lock or a handoff.
mapping.source_context: Review of juvenile rough-and-tumble play in laboratory rats; behavioural development plus mPFC/OFC neuronal anatomy.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (canonical review). Mapping fidelity good for the antecedent, weaker for the loop-level mechanism. Transfer risk moderate: rat->human is well-trodden for play, but the BG-commitment-loop reading is REE-specific.
